findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or replace `JIRA_BIND` in the AC5 immutability spec and 'What' guard paragraph: the event type does not exist in `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` or anywhere in the codebase; replace with the real event types whose processors might carry `creation_channel` in their payloads (e.g. SNAPSHOT restoration, or leave the list accurate to only CREATE/EDIT).
evidence: Grep of entire repo for 'JIRA_BIND' returns zero matches

KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES in src/rebar/reducer/_version.py (lines 112-148) contains CREATE, STATUS, EDIT, LINK, UNLINK, COMMENT, TAG_DELTA, KEY_ADD, KEY_REVOKE, COMMITS, WORKFLOW_RUN, WORKFLOW_STEP, SIGNATURE, ARCHIVED, SNAPSHOT — no JIRA_BIND
Plan quote: 'STATUS, LINK, JIRA_BIND, plus other specialized events also cannot replace genesis provenance' (AC5)
Previous plan-review cache at .rebar/cache/plan-review/6fe2-ab22-c231-4a41/ independently confirmed this across multiple reviewers
findings.finding: `JIRA_BIND` is named in the plan as a real existing event type whose processors must ignore `creation_channel` (both in the 'What' section and in AC5), but `JIRA_BIND` does not exist anywhere in the codebase — it is absent from `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` in `reducer/_version.py`, from the replay dispatch in `reducer/_replay.py`, and from every search across the repo.
findings.location: What section (immutable-field guard paragraph) and AC5: 'STATUS, LINK, JIRA_BIND, plus other specialized events also cannot replace genesis provenance'
scenarios: The AC5 test (`-k immutable`) will assert behaviour for a non-existent event type, making the test either dead (event never produced) or misleading about what immutability protection is actually needed.
If a future story introduces a Jira-binding event under a different name, the plan's guard specification will have silently under-specified the protection scope.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section and AC5, replace 'STATUS, LINK, JIRA_BIND, plus other specialized events' with 'STATUS, LINK, and other non-CREATE events'. If a Jira-binding event is intended for a future story, note it explicitly as out-of-scope rather than treating it as a currently-existing type.

findings.finding: `JIRA_BIND` is named in the plan as a real existing event type whose processor must ignore `creation_channel`, but it does not exist anywhere in the codebase — absent from `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES`, `_processors.py`, and every event-dispatch table.
findings.location: What section (immutable-field guard paragraph): 'STATUS, LINK, JIRA_BIND, plus other specialized events also cannot replace genesis provenance'; AC5: 'STATUS, LINK, JIRA_BIND, plus other specialized events also cannot replace genesis provenance'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `JIRA_BIND` with the actual event types emitted by the Jira reconciler (e.g. `BRIDGE_ALERT`, or clarify that no Jira-specific event type currently exists in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES and the guard only needs to cover EDIT/STATUS/LINK/UNLINK/etc.).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC6) that verifies docs/event-schema.md and docs/output-schemas.md document the six-value creation_channel enum and the true-only inference marker, e.g. via a grep/snapshot assertion or manual review step.
evidence: Files list entry: 'docs/event-schema.md and docs/output-schemas.md: local channel contract.'
No AC contains a verify step for documentation correctness or presence of the six-value enum in docs.
findings.finding: The Files section lists docs/event-schema.md and docs/output-schemas.md as deliverables, but no acceptance criterion verifies that the documentation was updated with the local channel contract.
findings.location: Files section (docs/event-schema.md and docs/output-schemas.md) vs Acceptance Criteria (all 7 ACs)
scenarios: An implementer ships code-passing tests but leaves documentation stale; the deliverable is silently omitted with no AC catching it.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC6 to include: 'docs/event-schema.md and docs/output-schemas.md document creation_channel (cli|mcp|python|jira|import|unknown) and creation_channel_inferred (Literal[true]). Verify by grep or doc-test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a validation step in AC6 (or a dedicated doc-contract test) that asserts event-schema.md and output-schemas.md contain the six-value enum and immutability language — e.g., a grep/snapshot check or a dedicated docs-contract test file.
evidence: Files list: 'docs/event-schema.md and docs/output-schemas.md: local channel contract'

AC6 proof: 'uv run pytest -q tests/interfaces/facades/test_creation_channel.py -k projections && uv run pytest -q tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_ticket_state.py ... && python -m rebar.schemas.gen_types --check'
AC6 text says 'docs expose the same six-value enum' but no test command validates markdown doc content
ABSENCE: no doc-linting or snapshot test for docs correctness
findings.finding: The docs update for event-schema.md and output-schemas.md is listed in the Files section but no AC includes a validation step that confirms the documentation correctly reflects the six-value enum and the immutability contract — the AC6 proof (pytest + gen_types --check) validates code artifacts only.
findings.location: Files section (docs/event-schema.md, docs/output-schemas.md) and AC6
scenarios: Docs are not updated or contain stale enum values (e.g., missing a value, wrong spelling); no AC catches this; auditors reading docs see incorrect contract.
gen_types --check passes but docs still describe the old behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a small pytest test in tests/interfaces/contracts/ (e.g., test_docs_creation_channel.py) that reads docs/event-schema.md and docs/output-schemas.md and asserts each of the six enum values (cli, mcp, python, jira, import, unknown) and the immutable-field guard are present; reference it in AC6's proof command.

findings.finding: The plan lists `docs/event-schema.md` and `docs/output-schemas.md` in the Files section as deliverables, but no AC requires verifying that those docs were updated. An agent could skip the documentation updates and all ACs would still pass.
findings.location: Files section and Acceptance Criteria (all ACs)

findings.finding: AC6 claims 'docs expose the same six-value enum' as part of its done definition, but the proof command (`uv run pytest ... && python -m rebar.schemas.gen_types --check`) contains no step that verifies docs/event-schema.md or docs/output-schemas.md content. An implementer could skip the documentation update entirely and still pass all listed AC6 checks.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6 proof command; Files — docs/event-schema.md and docs/output-schemas.md
findings.suggested_fix: Add a small contract test or shell assertion — e.g., `python -c "import pathlib; doc=pathlib.Path('docs/event-schema.md').read_text(); [assert v in doc for v in ('cli','mcp','python','jira','import','unknown')]"` — appended to the AC6 proof command, or extract it into tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_coverage.py which is already in the proof list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan that apply_inbound_records.py's `rebar.ensure_identity_for()` call site will produce creation_channel='python' for Jira ghost-mints until e622 ships, and note whether this is an acceptable transient pollution or whether a temporary override (e.g. passing `creation_channel='jira'` directly in this story) is required.
evidence: apply_inbound_records.py:69: `rebar.ensure_identity_for('jira', account_id, display_name or account_id, repo_root=repo_root)`
Plan What section: 'Add creation_channel="python" keyword parameters to create_identity_core and ensure_identity_for. Local library callers use that default'
Ticket comment acknowledges: 'ensure_identity_for default python would mis-attribute the apply_inbound_records Jira ghost-mint call site -> flagged for e622 to override to jira at that boundary'
Files section lists no entry for apply_inbound_records.py
The gap window is every reconciler run between this story and e622 shipping
findings.finding: apply_inbound_records.py calls rebar.ensure_identity_for() (a public library function that will acquire creation_channel='python' as its default) but is not listed in the Files section and is not updated in this story. Every Jira-originated ghost-mint between this story landing and e622 will be stamped creation_channel='python', which is factually incorrect. This is a concrete unenumerated consumer of the changed ensure_identity_for signature.
findings.location: Files section / src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_inbound_records.py line 69
scenarios: Jira reconciler runs inbound create, calls ensure_identity_for to mint a placeholder identity; that identity gets creation_channel='python' instead of 'jira', polluting the audit trail before e622 can correct it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Scope section explicitly naming apply_inbound_records.py as a call site that will produce incorrect attribution during the delivery window, and consider whether passing creation_channel='jira' at that one call site is safe to include in this story rather than waiting for e622.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_coverage.py` to the Files section with a note on whether it needs changes to assert the new six-value enum and inferred marker.
evidence: AC6: 'uv run pytest -q tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_ticket_state.py tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_outputs.py tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_coverage.py'
test_schema_coverage.py absent from the Files section
findings.finding: The AC6 proving command references `tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_coverage.py` but this file does not appear in the Files section. If the file is an existing test that needs updating to assert the new six-value enum fields, the missing Files entry means an executor may not know to update it and the proving command could pass on stale coverage.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC6; Files section (absence)
scenarios: test_schema_coverage.py exists but is not updated; it passes because it doesn't yet assert on creation_channel — AC6's proving command returns green without verifying the new fields are covered.

evidence: 5 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the gen_types.py extension for `{"const": true}`, either implement general `const` handling (mapping any JSON const value to `Literal[<value>]`) or add an explicit docstring/comment in `_pytype()` and `_def_pytype()` stating that only `{"const": true}` is intentionally supported for the creation_channel_inferred marker.
evidence: gen_types.py _pytype() handles 'enum', '$ref', 'type' but has no 'const' branch at all (lines 115–149)

workflow.v1/v2/v3.schema.json each use {"const": "1"}/{"const": "2"}/{"const": "3"} for schema_version — those schemas aren't in TOP_LEVEL_OBJECTS but demonstrate the general const pattern exists in the schema corpus
Plan says only: 'JSON Schema {"const": true} generates Literal[True]' — no mention of general const handling or explicit scoping note
ABSENCE: no comment in gen_types.py documenting that const is intentionally unsupported except for boolean-true
findings.finding: The plan extends gen_types.py to handle `{"const": true}` → `Literal[True]` as a one-off boolean case, but the existing codebase already uses `{"const": "1"}`, `{"const": "2"}`, `{"const": "3"}` string const discriminators in workflow schemas. Implementing only boolean-`true` const handling leaves an undocumented partial implementation of the `const` keyword that will mislead future schema authors who add a string-const field to a generator-covered schema.
findings.location: src/rebar/schemas/gen_types.py / What section: 'Extend gen_types.py so JSON Schema {"const": true} generates Literal[True]'
scenarios: A future schema author adds a string-const discriminator to a ticket_state property, runs gen_types, gets 'Any' instead of Literal['value'] with no warning, and ships a weaker type contract.
A reviewer sees the partial const handler and assumes const is fully supported, adding {"const": false} which silently maps to 'Any'.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_pytype()`, add a branch before the `jtype` check: `if 'const' in node: self._used_typing.add('Literal'); return f"Literal[{node['const']!r}]"`. This handles all scalar const values (string, bool, int, null) uniformly, costs ~2 lines, and closes the partial-implementation trap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either confirm `--check` already exists in gen_types.py or add its implementation to the What/Files section so AC6's proving command is executable.
evidence: "python -m rebar.schemas.gen_types --check" appears as a proving command in AC6
What section describes gen_types changes only as: 'Extend src/rebar/schemas/gen_types.py so JSON Schema {"const": true} generates Literal[True]' — no mention of --check mode
Files entry for gen_types.py says only 'render JSON Schema const values as Literal[...] before generated-type checks' — no --check flag described
findings.finding: The AC6 proving command `python -m rebar.schemas.gen_types --check` relies on a `--check` mode that is never described as part of this story's deliverables. The What section says only 'render JSON Schema const values as Literal[...]'; the Files section lists gen_types.py but specifies no `--check` flag implementation. If `--check` doesn't already exist in the codebase, the proving command silently fails to exercise the claim (or errors out), making AC6 unverifiable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC6; Files: src/rebar/schemas/gen_types.py
scenarios: The --check flag doesn't exist yet; the proving command either errors or returns exit 0 trivially, never verifying that generated types match the schema.
An executor implements gen_types changes but omits --check, passes all other ACs, and AC6's proving command is silently broken.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Add a --check flag to gen_types.py that re-runs generation in-memory and exits non-zero if the output differs from the committed types.py, enabling the AC6 proving command.' Add gen_types.py to the Files section with this behaviour noted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either enumerate all known create_core callers in the Files section, or add an explicit instruction (e.g., 'run `grep -r create_core src/ tests/` and update every hit') so no caller is silently missed.
evidence: 'Make composer.create_core require an internal keyword-only creation_channel with no default... Every existing caller is updated in this story.'
Files section enumerates only 6 Python source files as callers; no mention of test utilities or other scripts that may call create_core directly
findings.finding: The plan states 'every existing caller is updated in this story' when create_core gains a mandatory keyword-only `creation_channel` (no default, TypeError on omission), but the Files section lists only a subset of callers (composer.py, _lib_writes.py, _mcp_writes.py, idea.py, session_log.py, identity.py). Any caller not in that list — such as test helpers, internal automation scripts, or other commands — would break at runtime without any plan guidance.
findings.location: What section ('Every existing caller is updated in this story'); Files section
scenarios: An executor follows the Files list, misses an unlisted internal caller, ships a TypeError at runtime in a path not covered by the listed tests.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Files entry: 'Run grep -r "create_core" src/ tests/ to enumerate all call sites; update each with the appropriate creation_channel value before the PR is merged.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out plan or expand-contract sequence for the `create_core` signature break: either document the revert commit procedure, or temporarily retain a deprecated default (e.g., `creation_channel=_UNSET`) that logs a warning before the default is removed in a follow-up, so no caller is silently stranded.
evidence: 'Make composer.create_core require an internal keyword-only creation_channel with no default, so omission raises TypeError at the internal seam'
'Every existing caller is updated in this story.'
No rollback sequence, no temporary default-then-remove expand-contract step, and no back-out instructions appear anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan breaks the internal `create_core` signature (no-default keyword-only `creation_channel` raises TypeError on omission) and states 'Every existing caller is updated in this story,' but provides no explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing if the migration is incomplete, a caller is missed (e.g., in a plugin, a test helper, or a future dynamic call site), or the deployment needs to be reverted mid-story.
findings.location: What section: 'Make composer.create_core require an internal keyword-only creation_channel with no default' / Files: src/rebar/_commands/composer.py
scenarios: A call site is missed during refactor; CI catches it, but the branch is partially merged — no documented revert procedure.
A downstream consumer (test utility, script) outside the listed files calls create_core without the new argument; TypeError surfaces in production with no staged migration path.
The story is split across commits; an intermediate state where create_core requires the argument but not all callers are updated breaks the main branch until the full story lands.
findings.suggested_fix: Use a sentinel default for one release cycle: `def create_core(..., creation_channel=_UNSET)` where `_UNSET` triggers a deprecation warning and falls back to `'unknown'`, then remove the sentinel in the follow-up story. Alternatively, add a plan section: 'Rollback: revert PR #XYZ; no data migration needed since creation_channel is additive in storage.' Either satisfies the rubric's requirement for an explicit reversibility mechanism.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the explicit-boundary-threading decision (and rejection of stack inspection / context-local state) in a durable design artifact — ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or an update to an existing architecture doc — and add it to the Files list.
evidence: 'Explicit boundary threading is chosen because the interface knows the channel and the shared core does not. It is simpler and more testable than stack inspection or context-local state' — present in plan prose only
Files section lists docs/event-schema.md and docs/output-schemas.md but no ADR or CLAUDE.md update
This decision affects every future ingress boundary (including e622 and beyond) so it is a durable architectural constraint
findings.finding: The architectural decision to thread creation_channel explicitly through call signatures (rather than using stack inspection or context-local/thread-local state) is explained in the plan's Approach Rationale section but is not captured in any durable design document (ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update). The Files section lists no such artifact.
findings.location: Approach Rationale section; Files section (no ADR/CLAUDE.md entry)
scenarios: A future contributor adds a new ingress boundary (e.g., webhook, REST API) and doesn't realize explicit threading is the mandated pattern, falling back to stack inspection or a global context variable, breaking the audit invariant.
During an onboarding review, the architectural rationale is lost because it lives only in a story plan rather than in the codebase's design documentation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an entry to CLAUDE.md (or a new docs/adr/creation-channel-threading.md) summarizing: (1) creation_channel is always threaded explicitly from the ingress boundary, (2) stack inspection and context-local state are explicitly rejected, (3) the closed-enum vocabulary lives in common.schema.json, and (4) e622 is the designated owner for Jira/import inference. List the file in the plan's Files section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define — at least at the interface/seam level — how e622 replaces provisional creation_channel=unknown: name the event type or projection mechanism that transitions provisional unknown to an inferred value, confirm it is not blocked by _IMMUTABLE_EDIT_FIELDS, and document the ownership boundary between this story and e622 for that state transition.
evidence: 'Child e622 treats absent/None/unknown-without-marker as unresolved legacy input so positive Jira inference can replace this provisional fallback' — mechanism of replacement is unspecified
No event type or projection rule is named for how e622 transitions provisional unknown to an inferred value
The immutable-field guard (_IMMUTABLE_EDIT_FIELDS) would block an EDIT event from replacing it, yet some replacement must occur — the mechanism is contradictory or deferred without a defined seam
findings.finding: The lifecycle of the provisional creation_channel=unknown state for legacy CREATEs is incomplete: the plan defines how unknown is written (reducer projection for legacy CREATEs lacking the field) and states that e622 'can replace this provisional fallback,' but does not specify the replacement mechanism — whether e622 writes a new event, mutates compiled state, or applies a projection-layer override. This leaves the update ownership and concurrency semantics of provisional unknown undefined in the shared state lifecycle.
findings.location: What section: 'A legacy CREATE with no value provisionally projects creation_channel=unknown without creation_channel_inferred'; AC line 4: 'A legacy CREATE with no channel provisionally reduces to unknown without creation_channel_inferred, remaining eligible for e622 inference'
scenarios: e622 is implemented and assumes it can write an EDIT event with creation_channel, but the immutable-field guard silently drops it, leaving tickets permanently stuck at unknown.
Two concurrent readers see provisional unknown during an e622 inference window and make conflicting downstream decisions about channel attribution.
A future compaction story tries to fold the provisional unknown into canonical state but has no defined event type or marker to distinguish 'genuinely unknown' from 'not-yet-inferred.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the What or Scope section naming the replacement seam: e.g., 'e622 supplies creation_channel by writing a CHANNEL_INFERRED event (or a dedicated projection override), which is exempt from the immutable-field guard because it is not an EDIT event.' Alternatively, confirm that _IMMUTABLE_EDIT_FIELDS only applies to EDIT-type events and that a CHANNEL_INFERRED event type will be defined in e622, making this story's guard non-blocking for that path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/reducer/_state.py` to the Files section and specify whether `make_initial_state()` should seed `creation_channel=None` (so the `if field_name not in state` guard naturally protects it on new tickets before process_create runs) and whether `process_snapshot` needs a migration guard analogous to the `managed_refs` and `attestations` guards for legacy SNAPSHOTs lacking `creation_channel`.
evidence: src/rebar/reducer/_state.py make_initial_state() does not include creation_channel
process_edit at src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:494 uses `if field_name not in state: continue` — if creation_channel IS in state (set by process_create), this guard does NOT protect it
The plan adds _IMMUTABLE_EDIT_FIELDS as the real guard, but _state.py is absent from the Files list

process_snapshot at line 744-748 copies all compiled_state fields verbatim — a SNAPSHOT carrying creation_channel would re-seed it into state, which is correct, but make_initial_state seeding matters for the legacy-ticket (no-CREATE-channel) path
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `process_edit`'s existing `if field_name not in state: continue` guard will be supplemented by `_IMMUTABLE_EDIT_FIELDS` to protect `creation_channel`, but omits that this guard only works if `creation_channel` is ALSO added to `make_initial_state()` in `reducer/_state.py`. If `creation_channel` is seeded into state only by `process_create` (not in `make_initial_state`), the `if field_name not in state` guard would naturally block it on tickets replayed from a CREATE with no channel (legacy path) — but on tickets with a channel set, the `_IMMUTABLE_EDIT_FIELDS` frozenset is the only guard. `make_initial_state()` is currently not mentioned in the Files or What section.
findings.location: What section: 'Define _IMMUTABLE_EDIT_FIELDS = frozenset({"creation_channel", "creation_channel_inferred"}) in reducer/_processors.py'; Files section: no mention of reducer/_state.py
scenarios: If `reducer/_state.py` is not updated to seed `creation_channel` in `make_initial_state`, then for legacy tickets (no channel on CREATE) `creation_channel` will not be in state until `process_create` runs — but `process_snapshot` restores `compiled_state` verbatim, so a compacted legacy ticket still won't have the field. The plan's 'legacy CREATE with no value provisionally projects creation_channel=unknown' depends on either `make_initial_state` or `process_create` seeding a default — neither is specified for the no-value case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the What section that session_log.start() and session_log.append() gain a creation_channel parameter (defaulting to 'python') so that session_log_cli can pass 'cli' and the library facade can pass 'python' — matching the explicit treatment already given to create_identity_core and ensure_identity_for.
evidence: session_log.py line 135: `create_core('session_log', summary or _DEFAULT_TITLE, description='', repo_root=repo_root)` — direct call with no channel
Plan What: 'Add creation_channel=python keyword parameters to create_identity_core and ensure_identity_for' — explicit for identity, silent for session_log.start/append
Plan What: 'session-log library helpers accept an internal creation_channel keyword defaulting to python' — implies start/append gain the param but does not state it explicitly
_lib_writes.append_session_log calls session_log.append(); _lib_writes.start_session_log calls session_log.start(); session_log_cli calls start/append directly
findings.finding: session_log.start() and session_log.append() are shared library+CLI callsites that both call create_core directly. The plan lists session_log.py as a file to update and states 'CLI handlers pass cli; ... session-log library helpers accept an internal creation_channel keyword defaulting to python', but does not explicitly enumerate that start() and append() must themselves gain a creation_channel parameter — unlike create_identity_core and ensure_identity_for which are explicitly called out for signature extension in the What section.
findings.location: What section (session-log threading) / src/rebar/_commands/session_log.py
scenarios: Implementer updates create_core's signature (no default) but misses threading creation_channel through start()/append(), causing TypeError at runtime when session_log_cli or the library calls start()/append().
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Add creation_channel="python" keyword parameters to session_log.start() and session_log.append(); session_log_cli passes cli, library callers (_lib_writes.append_session_log / start_session_log) use the python default.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the `unknown` fallback projection applies to compacted (SNAPSHOTted) legacy tickets within this story, and if so add a process_snapshot migration guard (analogous to the existing managed_refs/attestations/keyring guards) that seeds creation_channel=unknown when compiled_state contains neither creation_channel nor creation_channel_inferred.
evidence: process_snapshot in _processors.py lines 744-792 restores all compiled_state keys verbatim and has no mention of creation_channel migration
make_initial_state() in _state.py does not include creation_channel, so a pre-feature SNAPSHOT will not have it in compiled_state, and process_create won't replay after a snapshot
The plan explicitly defers 'SNAPSHOT durability' to OUT-of-scope, but does not acknowledge that the `unknown` fallback for compacted legacy tickets is also deferred
Analogous migrations exist in process_snapshot for managed_refs, attestations, and keyring (lines 763-792), suggesting the pattern is well-understood and should be planned here too
findings.finding: The plan does not address the projection gap for already-compacted (SNAPSHOTted) legacy tickets within this story's scope. For a compacted ticket, process_snapshot restores compiled_state verbatim and process_create does not replay; consequently, no creation_channel key (not even the `unknown` fallback) is projected for those tickets until a SNAPSHOT-migration path fires. The plan's existing process_snapshot already handles analogous migration cases (managed_refs, attestations, keyring) via explicit guards in _processors.py, but this story's plan is silent on whether the same pattern is needed for creation_channel, leaving an observable state gap for compacted tickets.
findings.location: What section / 'Store creation_channel in CREATE.data… project it into compiled state exactly once' / 'A legacy CREATE with no value provisionally projects creation_channel=unknown'
scenarios: A team runs this story's code on a repo with many compacted tickets — those tickets show no creation_channel in show/list/search output, not even `unknown`, breaking the stated AC that 'a legacy CREATE with no channel provisionally reduces to unknown'
Facade tests that read compacted tickets after the feature ships may silently miss the field, causing the 'projections' AC test to have incomplete coverage
findings.suggested_fix: Add a guard in process_snapshot (after the existing managed_refs/attestations/keyring migration blocks): `if "creation_channel" not in compiled_state: state["creation_channel"] = "unknown"` — consistent with the existing pattern and enabling the `unknown` fallback for compacted legacy tickets within this story's boundary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `process_snapshot` migration guard for `creation_channel` (mirroring the `managed_refs`/`attestations`/`keyring` guards): when `creation_channel` is absent from a restored `compiled_state`, set `state['creation_channel'] = 'unknown'` so compacted legacy tickets satisfy the same 'absent → unknown' contract the plan promises for uncompacted ones.
evidence: process_snapshot (line 744) restores compiled_state verbatim with no creation_channel guard, unlike managed_refs (line 763), attestations (line 772), and keyring (line 791) which each have explicit absent-field migration handlers.
process_create (line 105) is the only place the 'absent → unknown' fallback is described, but it is skipped entirely when a SNAPSHOT short-circuits replay.
AC states: 'A legacy CREATE with no channel provisionally reduces to unknown without creation_channel_inferred' — this will silently NOT hold for any compacted ticket.
ABSENCE: plan does not mention adding a process_snapshot migration guard for creation_channel, unlike every other new field added to compiled state in this codebase.
findings.finding: The plan's provisional `creation_channel=unknown` fallback for legacy tickets fires inside `process_create`, but `process_create` is NOT invoked after a SNAPSHOT event short-circuits replay. Tickets compacted before this story ships will have SNAPSHOTs without `creation_channel` in `compiled_state`; `process_snapshot` restores that compiled_state verbatim, leaving `creation_channel` absent — not `unknown`. The existing codebase pattern for exactly this situation (managed_refs, attestations, keyring) is an explicit guard in `process_snapshot`: `if 'field' not in compiled_state: state['field'] = <fallback>`. The plan omits this guard entirely.
findings.location: What section / reducer/_processors.py: process_snapshot + process_create; AC line: 'A legacy CREATE with no value provisionally projects creation_channel=unknown without creation_channel_inferred'
scenarios: A ticket created before this story is later compacted. Its SNAPSHOT has no creation_channel. After deploying this story, rebar show/list/search returns a ticket with no creation_channel (not even 'unknown'), violating the AC and the audit contract.
compact-all is run during or after rollout — all old tickets get SNAPSHOTs without creation_channel, defeating the 'legacy → unknown' guarantee permanently until the SNAPSHOT is rebuilt.
findings.suggested_fix:
In `reducer/_processors.py::process_snapshot`, after the keyring migration block, add:
```python
# creation_channel migration (story <this>): a SNAPSHOT written before this
# field existed carries no creation_channel. Provision the same provisional
# fallback that process_create applies to a live legacy CREATE.
if 'creation_channel' not in compiled_state:
    state['creation_channel'] = 'unknown'
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the plan (and a code comment in `_snapshot_strip_keys`) that `creation_channel` must NOT appear in the strip list, so compaction preserves genesis provenance through the SNAPSHOT.
evidence: _snapshot_strip_keys() (compact.py line 127) explicitly lists fields excluded from SNAPSHOT compiled_state. creation_channel is not mentioned.
The plan defines _IMMUTABLE_EDIT_FIELDS but says nothing about compaction stripping — an omission that could become a bug if _snapshot_strip_keys is extended for other reasons.
ABSENCE: no plan text confirms that creation_channel will survive compaction, or that _snapshot_strip_keys needs no update.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `creation_channel` should be excluded from `_snapshot_strip_keys()` in `compact.py`. Currently, only `updated_at` and `signature` are stripped from compiled_state before persisting a SNAPSHOT. If `creation_channel` is projected into compiled_state (as the plan requires), it will automatically survive compaction — but if a future reader or the plan's own immutability logic strips it, compacted tickets will lose their genesis channel. The plan is silent on this interaction.
findings.location: Files section / src/rebar/_commands/compact.py: _snapshot_strip_keys(); What section: 'Store creation_channel in CREATE.data for every new local creation and project it into compiled state exactly once'
scenarios: A maintainer adding another strip key to _snapshot_strip_keys is not warned that creation_channel must remain — the plan provides no rationale anchoring it to that list.

findings.finding: AC6's proving command applies the `-k projections` filter globally to all pytest paths in the same invocation, meaning tests in `test_schema_ticket_state.py`, `test_schema_outputs.py`, and `test_schema_coverage.py` whose names do not contain 'projections' will be silently deselected. Pytest exits 0 with 'no tests ran' (or runs only a subset) from those contract files, so the command can pass green without actually exercising the contract-file coverage it claims to prove.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC6 proving command
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the single AC6 command with:
```
uv run pytest -q tests/interfaces/facades/test_creation_channel.py -k projections && \
uv run pytest -q tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_ticket_state.py tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_outputs.py tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_coverage.py && \
python -m rebar.schemas.gen_types --check
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or extend an AC to explicitly invoke the runtime/schema vocabulary-drift contract test (e.g. tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_coverage.py or a dedicated path), confirming the six-value enum in _version.py matches the JSON Schema enum.
evidence: WHAT: 'Add the matching runtime constant plus validate_creation_channel(...) helper to src/rebar/reducer/_version.py and a contract test that prevents runtime/schema vocabulary drift.'
AC4 verify command: 'uv run pytest -q tests/interfaces/facades/test_creation_channel.py -k "invalid or legacy"' — targets facade tests, not the _version.py contract test.
AC6 verify command lists tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_*.py files but not a _version.py-level vocabulary-drift test.
findings.finding: WHAT describes 'a contract test that prevents runtime/schema vocabulary drift' located in reducer/_version.py, but no AC explicitly names or invokes this test file, leaving the vocabulary-drift guard unverified by the acceptance suite.
findings.location: WHAT section para 2 ('a contract test that prevents runtime/schema vocabulary drift') vs Acceptance Criteria (all 7 ACs)
scenarios: The vocabulary-drift contract test is written and passes locally but is not included in any AC's pytest invocation, so a future PR that removes it would not break any stated criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC6, append the vocabulary-drift test invocation: 'uv run pytest -q tests/interfaces/contracts/test_creation_channel_vocab.py' (or equivalent path), and name that file in the Files section.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
